skill.name: pitchcraft
skill.description: Structured persuasion for tech leads, PMs, and founders—not activity logs. Five scenarios (kickoff, status update, wrap-up, investor pitch, solution selling) on one 5-part framework (Hook→Context→Proposal→Evidence→Ask). AI prompts for missing materials and audience context; pre-submit checklist. Claude Code plugin; Cursor, Codex, and chat via prompts.
skill.summary: Turn scattered notes into executive briefings that win decisions using a single 5-part persuasion framework across five scenarios.
skill.author: moshuying

security_audit.summary: PitchCraft is a writing/framework skill. Its only executable artifact is scripts/install.js, a transparent postinstall file copier that uses fs.mkdirSync and fs.copyFileSync to place SKILL.md into the standard agent skill directories under the user's home or project root. No network calls, no credential reads, no obfuscation, no eval. The static analyzer's 255 findings are dominated by false positives: markdown backticks flagged as Ruby/shell execution, Apache-2.0 license text flagged as 'weak cryptographic algorithm' (e.g. 'WITHOUT WARRANTIES'), the project's own GitHub URLs flagged as 'hardcoded URLs', Chinese UTF-8 text flagged as high-entropy binary content, and standard ~ paths flagged as 'hidden file access'. The install script is a legitimate npm postinstall pattern. No confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Postinstall script writes to user home directory
medium_findings.description: scripts/install.js runs automatically on `npm install` and copies SKILL.md into the user's home directory (e.g. ~/.claude/skills/pitchcraft/, ~/.cursor/skills/pitchcraft/) or the project root, depending on scope. The script is transparent in its actions (no network, no exec, no eval), and a `PITCHCRAFT_SKIP_INSTALL=1` escape hatch is provided. This is a normal npm postinstall pattern for agent skills, but users should know the install behavior runs on package install.

medium_findings.title: Install script reads npm environment variables
medium_findings.description: scripts/install.js reads process.env.npm_config_prefix, process.env.npm_config_global, process.env.INIT_CWD, and process.env.PITCHCRAFT_SKIP_INSTALL to decide install scope. This is standard npm postinstall behavior; no secrets are read or exfiltrated. The script never calls network APIs and never writes these values anywhere outside the local filesystem.

low_findings.title: Static analyzer flagged markdown backticks as shell backtick execution
low_findings.description: The scanner reported 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' on every markdown file because of inline code spans like `SKILL.md` and code blocks fenced with triple backticks. These are markdown syntax, not shell substitution. No shell evaluation occurs.

low_findings.title: Static analyzer flagged license and JSON text as weak cryptographic algorithm
low_findings.description: The scanner reported 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' on lines containing the words 'Source', 'WITHOUT WARRANTIES', 'Source form', etc. inside LICENSE, plugin.json, package.json, and SKILL.md. There is no cryptographic code anywhere in the repository. These are false positives from the Apache-2.0 license text and from JSON metadata fields.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are all references to the project's own repository
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings in .claude-plugin/plugin.json, package.json, LICENSE, and README files all point to https://github.com/moshuying/pitchcraft or the author profile. These are documentation metadata, not external network calls. The install.js script performs no network requests.

low_findings.title: PowerShell installation helper included in commands/pitchcraft.md
low_findings.description: The command file ships both bash and PowerShell installation snippets for cross-platform support. The PowerShell block only creates a directory and copies SKILL.md into the standard Claude skills path. It is a manual user-invoked install helper, not auto-executed code.

low_findings.title: High file entropy on Chinese-language files is a UTF-8 false positive
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged SKILL.zh-CN.md, README.zh-CN.md, and prompts/universal.zh-CN.md as high-entropy binary/encrypted content. CJK characters are multi-byte UTF-8, which inflates Shannon entropy. The files are plain markdown, verified by reading.

low_findings.title: DANGEROUS COMBINATION heuristic: filesystem + env + network is normal for an installer
low_findings.description: The combined heuristic fired because the install script touches env vars, the filesystem, and references URLs in metadata. The script itself performs no network requests; the URLs are static metadata. The combination is the standard npm postinstall pattern, not malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Postinstall writes skill files into user home
dangerous_patterns.description: scripts/install.js:99-100 joins os.homedir() with the agent config segments to choose the install target. The same logic uses process.env.INIT_CWD for project scope. This is bounded to well-known agent paths; no user-controlled path segments are passed to the target.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path traversal pattern in install.js uses fixed __dirname
dangerous_patterns.description: scripts/install.js:12 uses path.join(__dirname, '..') to reach the package root. Because the input is __dirname (resolved at install time by Node), there is no user-controlled path traversal. The path is static and safe.

content.user_title: Write executive briefings that get decisions made
content.value_statement: Most status updates and pitches describe work instead of persuading. PitchCraft turns scattered notes into Hook→Context→Proposal→Evidence→Ask briefings that win approval, funding, or renewals in five reusable scenarios.
actual_capabilities: Applies a single 5-part persuasion framework (Hook→Context→Proposal→Evidence→Ask) to five briefing types: project kickoff, status update, wrap-up/annual review, investor pitch, and solution selling.
Probes the user for missing inputs (audience role, prior context, metrics, risks, funding ask) before drafting instead of inventing them.
Generates section-by-section drafts in the user's chosen language (English or Simplified Chinese) with a pre-submit checklist of ten quality checks.
Provides ready-to-paste prompts for Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, and generic chat UIs so the framework works in any AI tool.
Includes a one-shot installer (scripts/install.js) that copies the SKILL.md into the standard agent skills directory for claude-code, cursor, or codex, with a PITCHCRAFT_SKIP_INSTALL=1 escape hatch.
Encodes writing rules such as 'risks need judgment', 'metrics need source and definition', and 'no homework for the audience' so each briefing lands a decision.
limitations: Does not actually run briefings, send emails, or schedule meetings; the agent that loads the skill does the drafting.
Cannot verify the user's claims about progress, metrics, or market size; it only structures what the user supplies.
No live data lookup; the kickoff template needs the user to provide market, competition, and team context before it can be filled.
Does not auto-install via npm without user consent; npm postinstall can be skipped with PITCHCRAFT_SKIP_INSTALL=1 or --skip-install.

use_cases.title: Tech lead preparing a milestone review
use_cases.description: An engineering manager loads pitchcraft, picks 'status update', and the skill probes for completed work, key metrics with sources, and open risks with mitigations—producing a 1-page briefing that lands a go/no-go decision instead of a vague 'things are progressing'.

use_cases.title: Founder raising a seed round
use_cases.description: A founder uses pitchcraft to draft the 'investor pitch' scenario: one-liner, market size, product, moat, traction, and funding ask. The framework forces quantified evidence and a specific ask, so investors leave with a clear next step.

use_cases.title: Pre-sales engineer writing a customer proposal
use_cases.description: A solutions engineer pastes customer background and pain into the skill, picks 'solution selling', and gets a proposal built on the customer's own language, with a POC plan and engagement model. Cross-team mentions acknowledge existing tools first to avoid political pushback.

prompt_templates.title: Quick status update
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pitchcraft. Scenario: status update. Audience: my VP of Engineering (technical, weekly syncs). This period I shipped X, Y, Z. Key metric: latency p95 dropped from 400ms to 220ms (Datadog, last 7 days). Top risk: dependency on team A's auth refactor slipped one week. Ask: confirm we can defer the rollout by 5 days.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner — single-period status update with one risk and one ask

prompt_templates.title: Project kickoff for leadership approval
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pitchcraft. Scenario: project kickoff. Audience: CTO and CFO. Background: enterprise customers churn at 18% on slow support response. Proposal: build an in-app triage agent; in scope — ticket classification and L1 deflection; out of scope — L2 escalation. Milestones: prototype in 6 weeks, GA in 12. Ask: 2 backend engineers and a $40k LLM budget for Q3.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate — kickoff that names a pain, scope boundary, and resource ask

prompt_templates.title: Investor seed pitch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pitchcraft. Scenario: investor pitch. Audience: seed-stage funds focused on dev tools. One-liner: we give mid-market engineering teams SOC2-ready audit trails without a GRC hire. Market: $4.2B DevSecOps spend, growing 14% YoY. Moat: 2 years of incident-response data and an exclusive partnership with Cloud Security Alliance. Traction: $220k ARR, 18 logos, 132% NRR. Ask: $2.5M seed at $15M post, 18-month runway.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced — full seed pitch with quantified traction and a specific ask

prompt_templates.title: Customer-facing solution proposal
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pitchcraft. Scenario: solution selling. Customer: 800-person fintech, current state is a 4-tool observability stack with $1.2M/year in license and 3-week incident MTTR. Pain in their words: 'we pay for five dashboards and still find the bug in Slack'. Our solution: one pane with trace-to-deploy correlation, 14-day onboarding POC. Ask: 6-week paid POC with success criteria and a $180k annual contract if the MTTR target drops below 5 days.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert — full proposal that mirrors the customer's own language and quantifies outcomes

output_examples.input: Scenario: status update. Audience: VP Engineering. Period: this sprint. Done: migrated billing to Stripe; latency p99 from 1.2s to 380ms (Grafana, prod). Risk: payments team OOO next week, no rollback owner.
output: Hook: Billing migration shipped; checkout p99 is now 380ms (was 1.2s) per Grafana prod panel.
Context: We had a working billing integration on Stripe before this sprint; this change is the new retry and idempotency layer.
Proposal: Cutover complete, monitoring on for 72 hours. Three new metrics are live in the #billing-alerts channel.
Evidence: 1.2s → 380ms p99; 0 failed webhooks in the last 48h; chargeback rate unchanged at 0.04%.
Ask: Need a rollback owner identified before Friday while the payments team is OOO—propose rotating to infra on-call.

output_examples.input: Scenario: investor pitch. Founder pitching seed. Product: SOC2 audit trail for engineering teams. Numbers: $220k ARR, 18 logos, 132% NRR, $15M post.
output: Hook: SOC2-ready audit trails without hiring a GRC team—$220k ARR and 132% NRR in 9 months.
Context: Mid-market eng teams face SOC2 in year two and average $180k in audit-plus-GRC salaries; current tools are spreadsheets and screenshots.
Proposal: Continuous evidence collection from GitHub, AWS, and Okta, mapped to SOC2 controls, with a one-click auditor export.
Evidence: Moat—two years of incident-response corpus and a Cloud Security Alliance co-marketing deal. Traction—$220k ARR, 18 logos including two Fortune 1000, 132% NRR, 9-month payback.
Ask: $2.5M seed at $15M post to fund go-to-market in the US mid-market and SOC2 Type II certification by Q2.

best_practices: Always load memory first: ask the user for prior context, audience profile, and format preferences before drafting, instead of guessing.
Stop after each framework part and ask 'Does this answer what they care about?'—this is the cheapest edit and prevents losing the audience by paragraph three.
Lead the briefing with material risks and bad news before wins; the skill's writing rules enforce this to keep trust intact.
Quantify every metric with source, period, and definition (for example, 'p99 380ms (Grafana prod, last 7 days)') so leadership does not have to re-derive the number.
anti_patterns: Sending a 'progress update' that lists activities and never names a decision, budget ask, or risk with mitigation—audiences treat this as noise and stop reading.
Burying slipped milestones at the end of the briefing; the framework requires risks and bad news early when they matter.
Skipping the audience probe and writing the same kickoff pitch for a CTO, a CFO, and a VP of Sales—each cares about a different evidence set.
Treating the framework as a fill-in-the-blanks form; the writing rules (their concerns first, risks need judgment) matter more than the section headings.

faq.question: Is pitchcraft an AI model or a writing framework?
faq.answer: It is a writing framework plus a set of prompts. It runs on top of the AI tool you already use—Claude, Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, or a generic chat UI—and gives that tool a structured way to produce executive briefings.

faq.question: What scenarios does pitchcraft cover?
faq.answer: Five: project kickoff, status update, wrap-up or annual review, investor pitch, and solution selling. All five use the same Hook→Context→Proposal→Evidence→Ask backbone; only the section emphasis changes.

faq.question: Does pitchcraft run a postinstall script?
faq.answer: Yes, scripts/install.js runs on `npm install` and copies SKILL.md into the standard agent skills directory (for example, ~/.claude/skills/pitchcraft/). The script performs no network calls, no eval, and can be disabled with PITCHCRAFT_SKIP_INSTALL=1 or the --skip-install flag.

faq.question: Can I skip the install and just use the prompts?
faq.answer: Yes. prompts/universal.md and .claude/skills/pitchcraft/SKILL.md work as plain markdown—you can paste them into any AI chat tool, or drop the file into the right skills directory manually.

faq.question: Does pitchcraft send my data anywhere?
faq.answer: No. The install script only reads npm-related environment variables (npm_config_prefix, INIT_CWD, PITCHCRAFT_SKIP_INSTALL) and writes SKILL.md into a local agent directory. There are no network calls in the skill or the installer.

faq.question: What languages are supported?
faq.answer: English (SKILL.md) and Simplified Chinese (SKILL.zh-CN.md). The installer accepts --lang zh-CN to copy the Chinese version into the target skills directory.
